content:
# Galatians Chapter 2 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/GAL_002_page_001.jpg

### Paul Accepted by the Apostles in Jerusalem

1. Then fourteen years later I went back to Jerusalem again, this time with Barnabas; and I took Titus along too.

2. I went there because God revealed to me that I should go. While I was there, I met privately with those considered to be leaders of the church and shared with them the message I had been preaching to the Gentiles. I wanted to make sure that we were in agreement, for fear that all my efforts had been wasted.

3. And they supported my position. They did not even demand that my companion Titus, who is a Gentile, be circumcised.

4. Even that question came up only because of some so-called believers who had been secretly brought in. They sneaked in to spy on the freedom we have in Christ Jesus and wanted to drag us back into slavery.

5. But we refused to give in to them for a single moment. We wanted to preserve the truth of the gospel for you.

6. And the leaders of the church had nothing to add to my message. By the way, their reputation as great leaders made no difference to me. God has no favorites.

7. Instead, they saw that God had given me the responsibility of preaching the gospel to the Gentiles, just as he had given Peter the responsibility of preaching to the Jews.

8. For the same God who worked through Peter as the apostle to the Jews also worked through me as the apostle to the Gentiles.

9. In fact, James, Peter, and John, who were known as pillars of the church, recognized the gift God had given me, and they accepted Barnabas and me as their partners. They agreed that we should go to the Gentiles while they continued their work with the Jews.

10. Their only suggestion was that we keep on helping the poor, which I have always been eager to do.

11. But when Peter came to Antioch, I had to oppose him to his face, for what he did was very wrong.

12. When he first arrived, he ate with the Gentile believers. But afterward, when certain friends of James came, Peter wouldn't eat with the Gentiles anymore. He was afraid of criticism from those who insisted on the necessity of circumcision.

13. As a result, the other Jewish believers followed Peter's hypocrisy, and even Barnabas was led astray by their hypocrisy.

14. When I saw that they were not following the truth of the gospel message, I said to Peter in front of everyone: "Since you, a Jew, have been living like a Gentile and not like a Jew, how can you now try to make these Gentiles follow Jewish traditions?"

15. You and I are Jews by birth, not outsiders and sinners.

16. Yet we know that a person is made right with God by faith in Jesus Christ, not by obeying the law. And we have believed in Christ Jesus, so that we might be made right with God because of our faith in Christ, not because we have obeyed the law. For no one will ever be made right with God by obeying the law.

17. But suppose we seek to be made right with God through faith in Christ and then are found guilty because we have discarded the law. Would that mean Christ has led us into sin? Absolutely not!

18. Rather, I am a sinner if I rebuild the old system of law I already tore down.

19. For when I tried to keep the law, it condemned me. So I died to the law — I stopped trying to meet all its requirements — so that I might live for God.

20. My old self has been crucified with Christ. It is no longer I who live, but Christ lives in me. So I live in this earthly body by trusting in the Son of God, who loved me and gave himself for me.

21. I am not one of those who treats the grace of God as meaningless. For if keeping the law could make us right with God, then there was no need for Christ to die.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 2 — "Because God revealed to me that I should go":** Paul is careful to say that even his second trip to Jerusalem was God-directed, not a summons from the Jerusalem leaders. He went there to coordinate, not to get permission. The distinction matters enormously for Paul's argument: he is an independent apostle working in partnership, not a subordinate reporting to headquarters.

**Verse 11-14 — The Antioch Incident:** This is one of the most dramatic moments in the New Testament. Peter had been eating with Gentile believers — a radical act that crossed Jewish purity boundaries. But when people from James arrived, Peter backed down. Paul calls this what it is: hypocrisy. The man who had personally received the vision of the clean and unclean animals (Acts 10) was now acting as if Gentiles were unclean again. Paul confronted him publicly because the betrayal was public.

**Verse 16 — "Made right with God":** dikaioō — this is Paul's central theological term. To be "justified" or "made right" means to be declared righteous, to be brought into right relationship with God. Paul says this happens through faith, not through law observance. He says it three times in one verse — that's how important it is.

**Verse 20 — "Crucified with Christ":** This is the most famous verse in Galatians, and it may be the most concise summary of Paul's entire theology. The old self — the self that tried to earn God's favor through law observance — has died. What lives now is Christ in Paul. It's not that Paul has no identity; it's that his identity is now constituted by a relationship, not by performance.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
